food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dish with red meat

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a nasi lemak entry (FOODON_00002572) but does not have a mutton-specific variant; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers nasi lemak as a dish category; mutton variant not separately coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for nasi lemak or nasi lemak with mutton.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi lemak with mutton, comprising approximately 200 g coconut rice, 100 g mutton curry, sambal, fried anchovies, roasted peanuts, half a hard-boiled egg, and cucumber slices, totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak with Meat — IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite; supplemented with HPB Singapore Foodo nasi lemak reference and USDA SR Legacy mutton curry component
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: IMR MyFCD provides nasi lemak with meat as a composite dish. HPB Foodo provides a Singapore nasi lemak reference. Mutton curry component cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy lamb/mutton cooked values. Final per-100 g values represent a weighted composite for a standard nasi lemak with mutton plate (450 g serving). Values are consistent with published Malaysian food composition data for this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice-based mixed dishes — analogous estimate based on coconut rice (nasi lemak base) GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and published Southeast Asian food GI studies
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Glycemic Index Research and GI News
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for nasi lemak with mutton was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the nasi lemak base (coconut-milk steamed rice), which has a published GI of approximately 52–58 in Southeast Asian GI studies (Augustin et al., peer-reviewed). The mutton curry component contributes protein and fat that moderately lower the overall glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 94.5 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 51.975, rounded to 52.0. This is a high glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal, fried anchovies, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit nasi lemak with mutton to occasional consumption due to its high sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g, ~1395 mg per standard 450 g plate), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycaemic index (55) but very high glycaemic load per serving (~52.0) from coconut rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise strict portion control with nasi lemak with mutton; despite its low GI (55), the very high glycaemic load per standard plate (~52.0) can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and the saturated fat from coconut milk may further impair insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut milk and mutton, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its saturated fat content (~4.1 g per 100 g, ~18.5 g per plate) from coconut milk and mutton, combined with high sodium, both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak with Mutton is a hearty Malaysian and Singaporean rice dish in which fragrant coconut-milk steamed rice — infused with pandan leaf and lemongrass — is served alongside a slow-cooked mutton curry, spicy sambal chilli paste, crispy fried anchovies (ikan bilis), roasted peanuts, sliced cucumber, and a hard-boiled egg. The coconut rice is rich, slightly sweet, and aromatic, while the mutton curry adds deep savoury umami and warmth from spices such as cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli. The dish is a complete one-plate meal that is popular across Malaysia and Singapore, particularly in Malay and Indian-Muslim (Mamak) food stalls and restaurants. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~195 kcal per 100 g) with moderate protein (~8.5 g per 100 g) from mutton and egg, significant saturated fat (~4.1 g per 100 g) from coconut milk and mutton, and a low glycaemic index (~55) driven by the coconut rice base, but a very high glycaemic load per serving (~52.0). Sodium is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g) due to sambal and anchovies. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, saturated fat, and glycaemic load profile. It is naturally gluten-free and halal-certified in most preparations.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite data for nasi lemak with meat and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy for the mutton component. No single approved source provides an exact match for nasi lemak with mutton as a named dish entry; values represent a defensible adapted composite. GI is an analogous estimate from published coconut rice GI data. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo source databases for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary regional sources for this dish. All other -1 values have been replaced with estimated or source-derived numeric values where available. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B vitamins, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc are estimated from IMR MyFCD composite and USDA SR Legacy mutton cooked values. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on coconut rice (nasi lemak base) GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Southeast Asian GI studies; the mutton curry component moderates the overall glycaemic response. GL per serving = (55 × 94.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 52.0. Cuisine tags from the input (Noodle, Fried Rice, Roti Prata) appear to be restaurant-level tags from field survey and are not relevant to this specific dish; they have been excluded from the output. The field dataed (Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant - Circular Road) is noted as the source context but is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
